papers.title: The properties of hypervelocity stars and S-stars originating from an eccentric disc around a supermassive black hole

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) that are observed in the Galactic halo, are believed to be accelerated to large velocities by a process of tidal disruption of binary stars passing close to a supermassive black hole (SMBH) which resides in the center of the Galaxy. It is, however, still unclear, where these relatively young stars were born and which dynamical process pushed them to nearly radial orbits around the SMBH. In this paper we investigate the possibility that the young binaries originated from a thin eccentric disc, similar to the one observed in the Galactic center nowadays. By means of direct N-body simulations, we follow the dynamical evolution of an initially thin and eccentric disc of stars with a 100% binary fraction orbiting around the SMBH. Such a configuration leads to Kozai-Lidov oscillations of orbital elements, bringing considerable amount of binaries to close vicinity of the black hole. Subsequent tidal disruption of these binaries accelerates one of their component to velocities well above the escape velocity from the SMBH while the second component becomes tightly bound to the SMBH. We describe the main kinematic properties of the escaping and tightly bound stars within our model and compare them qualitatively to the properties of the observed HVSs and S-stars, respectively. The most prominent feature is a strong anisotropy in the directions of the escaping stars which is observed for the Galactic HVSs but not explained yet.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper presents $N$-body simulations of a thin, eccentric stellar disc around a supermassive black hole with 100% binary fraction, demonstrating that Kozai-Lidov oscillations induced by disc self-gravity drive significant binary tidal disruptions. These disruptions produce hypervelocity stars (HVSs) with strong directional anisotropy and tightly bound S-stars, matching observed kinematic properties—offering a physically motivated origin for both populations without requiring external massive clusters or pre-injected binaries.

source_fields.title: The properties of hypervelocity stars and S-stars originating from an eccentric disc around a supermassive black hole

source_fields.sections_text: 3. Results: In model A, approximately 12 hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are produced per simulation on average, with the Hills mechanism accounting for about 83% of ejections and dominating the velocity distribution at $v \\gtrsim 500\\,\\mathrm{km\\,s}^{-1}$, leading to HVSs at $50\\,\\mathrm{kpc}$ that closely match observed velocity distributions. 4. Discussion: A simple model shows that S-stars and HVSs may originate from a fragmented eccentric stellar disc around a supermassive black hole, with binary evolution and Kozai-Lidov mechanisms driving tidal breakup and ejection, and kinematic properties of the resulting stars qualitatively match observations—though uncertainties in observational data and binary merger rates (ranging from a few percent to ~10%) or the damping effects of the nuclear star cluster complicate the model's accuracy and require future observations for validation. 5. Conclusions: This model explains the origin of HVSs and S-stars through tidal break-ups of binaries in an eccentric stellar disc driven by the Kozai-Lidov mechanism, predicting a strongly anisotropic HVS distribution and burst-like formation, while noting limitations in observational verification and the potential for some S-stars to originate from the current young disc. 1. Introduction: This paper proposes a novel mechanism—Kozai-Lidov resonance driven by a secondary, inclined stellar disc—to explain how binaries in the Galactic center's young stellar disc can achieve high eccentricities, enabling them to plunge below the tidal break-up radius and form both S-stars and hypervelocity stars. 2. Model and method: The paper proposes a numerical model of gravitationally interacting point masses in the Galactic center, where stars and a supermassive black hole are placed on elliptical orbits with aligned eccentricities and specific initial distributions to study how the Kozai-Lidov mechanism can produce high-velocity stars and S-stars through secular orbital evolution.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical N-body simulation paper investigating formation mechanisms. Abstract and TLDR describe modeling and comparing to observed properties qualitatively, but no presentation or analysis of new or existing object-level observational data.

papers.title: Star Clusters and Super Massive Black Holes: High Velocity Stars Production

abstract.text: One possible origin of high velocity stars in the Galaxy is that they are the product of the interaction of binary systems and supermassive black holes. We investigate a new production channel of high velocity stars as due to the close interaction between a star cluster and supermassive black holes in galactic centres. The high velocity acquired by some stars of the cluster comes from combined effect of extraction of their gravitational binding energy and from the slingshot due to the interaction with the black holes. Stars could reach a velocity sufficient to travel in the halo and even overcome the galactic potential well, while some of them are just stripped from the cluster and start orbiting around the galactic centre.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper presents a dynamical model showing that star clusters—particularly globular clusters (GCs) and young stellar clusters (YC)—interacting with supermassive black holes (SMBHs) or black hole binaries (BHBs) in galactic centers can produce high-velocity stars (HVSs). The ejection efficiency depends on cluster mass, orbital eccentricity, and the BHB mass ratio, with a significant enhancement in hyper-velocity star (HyVS) production when the BHB mass ratio exceeds 1/20. Young clusters in elliptical orbits generate jets of HyVSs, with ~0.1% of stars reaching escape velocities, including binary systems that may evolve into blue stragglers.

source_fields.title: Star Clusters and Super Massive Black Holes: High Velocity Stars Production

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to infer Galactic gravitational potential and dark matter distribution by analyzing Hyper Velocity Stars, addressing the problem of directly probing extreme astrophysical environments and their underlying dynamics. HVSs from Star Clusters: This section demonstrates that both globular clusters and young stellar clusters can produce high-velocity stars (HVSs) through gravitational interactions with supermassive black holes or binary black hole systems, with the ejected star fraction and velocity distributions depending on cluster mass, orbital parameters, and black hole properties.

title: Star Clusters and Super Massive Black Holes: High Velocity Stars Production

catalog_assessment.evidence: Dynamical modeling paper proposing a new production channel via cluster-BH interactions. Abstract and TLDR describe theoretical mechanisms and ejection efficiencies, with no mention of compiling, analyzing, or presenting observational data for specific stars.

papers.title: A Dipole on the Sky: Predictions for Hypervelocity Stars from the Large Magellanic Cloud

abstract.text: We predict the distribution of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), under the assumption that the dwarf galaxy hosts a central massive black hole (MBH). For the majority of stars ejected from the LMC the orbital velocity of the LMC has contributed a significant fraction of their galactic rest frame velocity, leading to a dipole density distribution on the sky. We quantify the dipole using spherical harmonic analysis and contrast with the monopole expected for HVSs ejected from the Galactic Center. There is a tendril in the density distribution that leads the LMC which is coincident with the well-known and unexplained clustering of HVSs in the constellations of Leo and Sextans. Our model is falsifiable, since it predicts that Gaia will reveal a large density of HVSs in the southern hemisphere.

brief.tldr: [theoretical astrophysics / galactic dynamics] The paper models hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) due to tidal disruption near a central massive black hole, predicting a sky-dipole density distribution caused by the LMC's orbital velocity vector. This dipole explains the observed clustering of HVSs in Leo and Sextans, with a predicted high-density tendril in the southern hemisphere that is falsifiable via Gaia data; the model contrasts with a monopole distribution expected from Galactic Center ejections and includes velocity evolution through the Milky Way's gravitational potential.

source_fields.title: A Dipole on the Sky: Predictions for Hypervelocity Stars from the Large Magellanic Cloud

source_fields.sections_text: 2. Method: The paper proposes a simulation method to predict hypervelocity star (HVS) observables from the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) by modeling stars ejected via the Hills mechanism from a central black hole, with their initial velocities derived from orbital dynamics and evolved through the Milky Way's gravitational potential to compare with Galactic Center HVS populations. 3. Discussion: The LMC-origin hypervelocity stars exhibit a dipolar sky distribution concentrated near Leo and Sextans, contrasting with the isotropic distribution of Galactic Center-origin stars, and show a dense clump in the southern hemisphere consistent with known surveys and HVS11's proximity to Sextans. 4. Conclusions: Hypervelocity stars originating in the Large Magellanic Cloud, particularly through mechanisms like the Hills mechanism or runaway binaries, may explain the clustering of HVSs toward Leo and Sextans, with future surveys like SkyMapper and Gaia enabling robust testing and detailed analysis of their sky distributions. 1. Introduction: This paper proposes that the spatial anisotropy of Milky Way hypervelocity stars arises from their ejection from the Large Magellanic Cloud via the Hills mechanism, offering a novel explanation for their observed sky distribution by linking LMC dynamics to MW HVS formation.

title: A Dipole on the Sky: Predictions for Hypervelocity Stars from the Large Magellanic Cloud

catalog_assessment.evidence: Theoretical prediction paper modeling sky distribution of HVSs from LMC. Mentions observed clustering in Leo/Sextans as motivation and makes falsifiable predictions for Gaia, but does not present, compile, or analyze an object-level observational catalog itself.
